title: When the Bear Comes Back Again
content:
آ آ آ آ Oh, the scene is wide anï؟½ dreary anï؟½ the sun is settinï؟½ red,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ the grey-black sky of winterï؟½s cominï؟½ closer overhead.
آ آ آ آ Oh, the sun is settinï؟½ bloody with a blood-line on the snow,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ across it to the westward you can see old Bruin go;
آ آ آ آ You can see old Shaggy go,
آ آ آ آ You can see the brown Bear go,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ heï؟½s dragginï؟½ one leg arter, anï؟½ heï؟½s travellinï؟½ pretty slow.

آ آ آ آ We can send a long shot arter, but he doesnï؟½t seem to know,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a thin red line behind him where itï؟½s dripped across the snow;
آ آ آ آ He is weary anï؟½ heï؟½s wounded, with his own blood heï؟½s half-blind,
آ آ آ آ He is licked anï؟½ heï؟½s defeated, anï؟½ heï؟½s left some cubs behind;
آ آ آ آ Yes, heï؟½s left some cubs behind;
آ آ آ آ Oh, heï؟½s left some cubs behind;
آ آ آ آ To the tune of sixty thousand he has left some cubs behind.

آ آ آ آ Oh, theyï؟½ve pulled him by the nose-ring and theyï؟½ve baited him in pits,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ they bluffed him, anï؟½ they bruised him, anï؟½ they mostly gave him fits;
آ آ آ آ But he hugged ï؟½em badly one time when they tried him in his den,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ heï؟½ll make it warm for someone when he comes back East again;
آ آ آ آ When the Bear comes back again,
آ آ آ آ When heï؟½s lopinï؟½ round again,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½ll be lively times for Jacko when the Bear comes back again.

آ آ آ آ Oh, we chased him out of Turkey, I donï؟½t know for what idea,
آ آ آ آ It took two dogs anï؟½ a lion for to beat him in Crimea;
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s goinï؟½ home to lick his wounds, heï؟½s goinï؟½ to his den,
آ آ آ آ But heï؟½ll make it warm for someone when he comes South-East again,
آ آ آ آ When the Bear comes back again,
آ آ آ آ When old Bruin comes again,
آ آ آ آ He will make some dead to die on when he comes back from his den.

آ آ آ آ Keep a sharp look-out behind you, every way you turn, my lad,
آ آ آ آ It donï؟½t matter who you might be, for you bet the Bear is mad;
آ آ آ آ Keep a sharp look-out to Norï؟½ard, to the South anï؟½ West anï؟½ East,
آ آ آ آ For he mostly always finds you where you most expect him least;
آ آ آ آ Where you most expect him leastest,
آ آ آ آ Where you most expect him least,
آ آ آ آ Oh, youï؟½ll catch him grabbinï؟½ for yer where you most expect him least.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
